aliases: Roti John with Mutton and Cheese

english: Roti John with Mutton and Cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Roti John; 'sandwich food product' is the closest defensible parent class. Roti John is a Southeast Asian open-faced sandwich made with baguette, egg, and filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Sandwich) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No specific Roti John code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Roti John or equivalent Southeast Asian sandwich dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Roti John Mutton with Cheese is approximately 200 g, representing one full baguette portion with mutton filling, egg, and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Roti John Mutton W Cheese — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roti John Mutton W Cheese (cr_id G00010885). All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roti John Mutton with Cheese. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate carrier being a white baguette/French bread, which has a GI of approximately 65–70 per University of Sydney GI Database (white bread reference). The protein and fat content from mutton, egg, and cheese will attenuate the glycemic response in practice. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 17.7) / 100 ≈ 11.5, using 200 g serving size (8.85 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Roti John Mutton with Cheese; the sodium content of 1279 mg/100 g (approximately 2558 mg per typical 200 g serving) far exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg and is driven by processed cheese, condiments, and added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White baguette provides high-GI carbohydrates (GI ~65); mitigated by high protein (16.1 g/100 g) and fat content which slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Roti John Mutton with Cheese in moderation; the white baguette base contributes medium-GI carbohydrates, but the high protein from mutton, egg, and cheese helps blunt the postprandial glucose rise — request wholemeal bread and avoid sweet chilli sauce to further reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~10.2 g/100 g) from processed cheese and mutton, combined with very high sodium (1279 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (78 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Roti John Mutton with Cheese; the combination of high saturated fat from cheese and mutton, elevated sodium, and dietary cholesterol collectively raises LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk — if consumed, reduce cheese portion and avoid high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid precursor), high saturated fat from cheese, pan-frying generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and high sodium worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Roti John Mutton with Cheese; mutton is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways, saturated fat from cheese exceeds 5 g/100 g, and pan-frying generates AGEs — substituting mutton with chicken or fish and grilling instead of frying would substantially reduce the pro-inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roti John Mutton with Cheese is a popular Singaporean street food and hawker dish consisting of a halved French baguette that is pan-fried with a spiced minced mutton and egg mixture pressed onto the cut surface, then topped with melted processed cheese and served with chilli sauce and mayonnaise. The dish originated in Singapore's Malay-Muslim hawker culture and is commonly found at pasar malam (night markets), hawker centres, and food courts across the island. The texture combines a crispy, egg-coated bread exterior with a savoury, slightly spiced mutton filling and a rich, creamy cheese layer. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 307 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 23.1 g/100 g (predominantly from cheese and mutton), substantial protein at 16.1 g/100 g, and relatively low carbohydrate at 8.9 g/100 g. The sodium content is very high at 1279 mg/100 g, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The calcium content is notably elevated at 1375 mg/100 g, largely attributable to the processed cheese component. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker preparations. It is typically consumed as a lunch or dinner item and is considered a comfort food with strong cultural identity in Singapore's Indian-Muslim culinary heritage.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical entry for Roti John Mutton W Cheese (cr_id G00010885) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analysis of processed cheese and mutton proportions as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source and is plausible given the white baguette base and processed filling components. 2. saturated_fat_g (10.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for processed cheese (approximately 60% of fat as saturated) and mutton, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Roti John preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on white baguette/French bread GI (~65) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Roti John Mutton with Cheese exists in the published literature. 5. The calcium value of 1375 mg/100 g is unusually high and is preserved from the HPB source; this likely reflects a high proportion of processed cheese in the dish formulation tested by HPB. 6. Halal status is based on typical hawker preparation using halal-certified mutton and cheese; individual vendor certification should be verified.
